title: Peeking at the Top
subtitle: Preview files with head

sections.content: Sometimes a file is long and you only need to see the **beginning**. The `head` command shows just the first few lines.
sections.analogy: It's like reading the first paragraph of a long article to see if it's what you're looking for.
keyPoints: `head` shows the first 10 lines by default
Perfect for previewing large files without overwhelming your screen

sections.instruction: That log file has 50 lines. Using `cat` would flood your screen. `head` shows just the first 10 lines by default — perfect for a quick peek.
sections.prompt: Type `head log.txt` to see the first 10 lines of the server log.

onSuccess.message: Just the first 10 lines! You can see the server starting up: loading config, connecting to the database, registering routes. Much better than scrolling through all 50 lines.

sections.instruction: You can control exactly how many lines `head` shows with the `-n` flag. Want just the first 3? Use `-n 3`.
sections.prompt: Type `head -n 3 log.txt` to see only the first 3 lines.

onSuccess.message: Only 3 lines — the very start of the server boot. The `-n` flag lets you be precise about how much you want to see.

sections.instruction: Here's a pro trick: use `head -n 1` on a CSV file to see just the column headers. That tells you what data the file contains without drowning in rows.
sections.prompt: Type `head -n 1 data.csv` to see the column headers.

onSuccess.message: name, email, role, joined — now you know the structure of the data without reading all 30 rows. Developers do this constantly with CSV and log files.

sections.prompt: To see only the first 5 lines of a file, you type: head ___ 5 filename

completionMessage: You now know `head` — perfect for peeking at the beginning of files. Use `head` for a quick 10-line preview, or `head -n N` for exactly N lines. But what about the end of a file?
